These are held out of the per-letter corpus and the regression, and reported as a separate opposition tally, side by side with the individually-classified corpus and never summed with it (anonymous aggregate counts may overlap with named corpus letters). 2026-06-18: Types D and E promoted. 2026-06-22: Type G added. 2026-06-24: Type H promoted (56) and new Type I added (800); A refreshed 8,600->16,352 and F 18,302->19,077; total -> 36,533...

types.text:
Dear Commissioners,
Please keep quarterly reporting requirements in place.
Thanks,

rater_notes.primary: Oppose — a request to keep quarterly reporting in place is opposition to the move to optional semiannual (Form 10-S).
rater_notes.literalist: Oppose — explicit instruction to retain the existing quarterly requirement; status-quo preservation is opposition to the change.
rater_notes.skeptic: Oppose — no qualification, concession, or alternative cadence; nothing to flip to Conditional.

types.text:
Dear SEC Officials,
As an investor, I oppose this rule change.
Regards,

rater_notes.primary: Oppose — explicit 'I oppose this rule change.'

types.text: I strongly oppose moving to semiannual reporting.

Dear Secretary,
I am writing to express my strong opposition to the Securities and Exchange Commission's proposed rule that would allow public companies to opt out of quarterly reporting (Form 10-Q) in favor of semiannual reporting on the proposed Form 10-S. While the Commission frames this as a measure to reduce regulatory burdens and curb corporate "short-termism," the actual consequence will be a severe erosion of market transparency that disproportionately harms retail investors while providing an unfair advantage to institutional "big fish."
The proposed rule undermines the core mission of the SEC—to protect investors and maintain fair, orderly, and efficient markets—in the following ways:

### 1. Widening the Information Asymmetry Gap
The public markets are supposed to operate on the principle of a level playing field. Moving from a 90-day reporting cadence to a 180-day cadence creates a massive data vacuum. Six months is an eternity in the modern economy; supply chain disruptions, shifts in consumer demand, and sudden fiscal pressures can fundamentally alter a company's financial health in a fraction of that time.
While everyday investors rely heavily on public periodic filings to evaluate their holdings, institutional investors, hedge funds, and large private equity firms do not. "Big fish" possess the capital and resources to bridge a six-month information gap by purchasing alternative data streams, hiring private research firms, utilizing satellite tracking, and maintaining direct lines of communication with corporate management. Retail investors will be left entirely in the dark, forced to make decisions using stale financial data while Wall Street operates with near-real-time insights.

### 2. Over-Reliance on Form 8-K and the Failure of Regulation FD
The proposal suggests that event-driven disclosures (Form 8-K) and Regulation FD will adequately protect investors between semiannual reports. This is a flawed assumption. Form 8-K is triggered by specific, discrete material events (e.g., bankruptcy, executive turnover, major acquisitions). It does not capture the slow, cumulative erosion of profit margins, rising inventory costs, or deteriorating cash flows that are meticulously detailed in a Form 10-Q. Without granular quarterly financial statements, retail investors will lack the tools to diagnose structural corporate decline until it is too late.

### 3. Increased Market Volatility and Risk
Fewer reporting intervals mean that when financial data is finally disclosed every six months, the market reactions will be significantly more volatile. Instead of adjusting stock prices incrementally based on quarterly performance, the market will endure massive, unpredictable swings as six months of accumulated, unpriced operational realities are dumped onto the public at once. Retail investors, who generally lack the sophisticated hedging strategies and algorithmic execution speeds of institutional traders, will bear the brunt of this heightened volatility.

### Conclusion
Reducing corporate compliance costs should never come at the expense of market integrity and investor protection. If adopted, this rule will effectively re-privatize public market information, pricing out the everyday investor and turning back the clock on decades of transparency reforms.
I strongly urge the Commission to withdraw this proposal and maintain mandatory quarterly reporting on Form 10-Q. The health of our public markets depends on equal, timely access to information for all participants, not just the wealthiest institutions.
Sincerely,

rationale_evidence.IA: Widening the Information Asymmetry Gap — 'big fish' bridge the six-month gap with alternative data/satellite/management access while retail are left in the dark with stale data
rationale_evidence.MF: data vacuum + massive, unpredictable price swings as six months of unpriced operational realities are dumped at once (price discovery / volatility)
rationale_evidence.IP: undermines the SEC's core mission to protect investors; 're-privatize public market information', turning back decades of transparency reforms
rationale_evidence.CMP: granular quarterly financial statements — Form 8-K captures discrete events but not the slow erosion of margins, inventory, cash flows detailed in a 10-Q

rater_notes.primary: Oppose — 'strong opposition'; structured three-pillar argument (information asymmetry, 8-K/Reg-FD inadequacy, volatility) and a withdraw demand.
rater_notes.literalist: Oppose — 'I strongly urge the Commission to withdraw this proposal and maintain mandatory quarterly reporting.'
rater_notes.skeptic: Oppose — engages the Commission's burden/short-termism framing only to rebut it; NO concession, alternative cadence, or hedge — nothing to flip to Conditional (contrast Type E).
rater_notes.rationale: Substantive template; codes IA (big-fish vs retail), MF (data vacuum/volatility/price discovery), IP (investor-protection mission / re-privatization), CMP (granularity — 8-K vs 10-Q detail). RI (reliance on periodic filings) + CB (compliance-cost framing, rebutted) inclusive-only.

types.text:
Dear SEC Officials,
I am writing to oppose the proposed rule that would allow public companies to replace quarterly Form 10-Q reporting with semiannual reporting on Form 10-S.
I understand the concern that quarterly reporting can contribute to short-termism, managerial distraction, compliance costs, and an unhealthy culture of earnings theater. Those concerns should be taken seriously. I also recognize that not every company is situated identically. Smaller companies, pre-revenue companies, long-horizon companies, and companies in highly cyclical industries may experience quarterly reporting differently than large, mature issuers.
I would support a serious effort to reduce unnecessary reporting burden and short-term earnings pressure, especially for smaller issuers, so long as that effort preserves timely, standardized public information. But reducing standardized public disclosure from quarterly to semiannual reporting is too blunt a remedy.
The problem is not simply how often companies report. The deeper question is who has access to reliable information, when they receive it, and whether the public market remains a genuinely public field of visibility.
Quarterly reporting is not a perfect solution to the deeper problems of financialized capitalism. It does not by itself create justice, shared flourishing, or democratic accountability. But in a market system in which ordinary people's retirement savings, pensions, public institutions, and communities are already deeply entangled, timely standardized disclosure remains an important accountability structure.
If required reporting moves from quarterly to semiannual, the period between official public financial updates doubles. During that longer gap, material business realities may develop well before ordinary investors receive a full public accounting. Insiders, large institutions, and professional market participants may still have access to alternative data, management access, analyst networks, industry intelligence, and other channels. Ordinary investors are more dependent on standardized public filings.
Reducing quarterly disclosure does not eliminate information. It risks redistributing informational advantage toward those already best positioned to obtain it.
The Commission should therefore distinguish between factual quarterly reporting and the unhealthy practices sometimes associated with quarterly capitalism. Quarterly earnings guidance, short-term compensation incentives, analyst pressure, and performative earnings management may all deserve scrutiny. But the remedy for those problems should not be to dim one of the few standardized sources of public information available to all investors at the same time.
If the Commission believes quarterly reporting is too burdensome, it should consider narrower reforms: simplifying Form 10-Q where appropriate, scaling requirements for smaller issuers, discouraging or regulating quarterly earnings guidance, improving material-event disclosure, or addressing incentive structures that reward short-termism. Those approaches would target the problem more directly while preserving the public transparency floor that ordinary investors rely on.
I am also concerned about comparability. If some companies report quarterly while others report semiannually, investors may find it harder to compare companies within the same sector, assess risk in real time, and distinguish genuine long-term strategy from avoidable opacity. Market discipline may not be sufficient to protect ordinary investors, because sophisticated actors can price and navigate reduced disclosure more effectively than retail investors can.
The burden should therefore rest on proponents of the proposed change to show that ordinary investors will not be harmed, that market transparency will not be meaningfully reduced, that liquidity and price discovery will not suffer, and that any compliance savings outweigh the public cost of less frequent standardized disclosure.
I urge the Commission not to adopt the proposed rule as written. At minimum, the Commission should preserve quarterly factual reporting as the default public transparency floor unless it can demonstrate, with strong evidence, that any alternative reporting regime will protect ordinary investors, preserve comparability, maintain timely access to material information, and avoid widening the gap between insiders and the public.
Transparency will not solve the deeper inequities of public markets. But reducing standardized public disclosure would increase the risk of informational domination. The Commission should not make public markets darker for the people least able to see through them.

rationale_evidence.IA: redistributing informational advantage toward those already best positioned; insiders/large institutions have alternative data while ordinary investors depend on standardized public filings
rationale_evidence.IP: preserve quarterly factual reporting as the default public transparency floor that ordinary investors rely on
rationale_evidence.CMP: harder to compare companies within the same sector if some report quarterly and others semiannually
rationale_evidence.MF: liquidity and price discovery will not suffer; assess risk in real time
rationale_evidence.AL: narrower reforms instead — simplify Form 10-Q, scale requirements for smaller issuers, regulate quarterly earnings guidance

rater_notes.primary: Oppose — dominant, repeated position is opposition to the rule as written ('I urge the Commission not to adopt'); the narrower-reform suggestions are substitute remedies, not conditional acceptance of semiannual.
rater_notes.literalist: Oppose — explicit 'I am writing to oppose' and 'I urge the Commission not to adopt the proposed rule as written.'
rater_notes.skeptic: Conditional — heavily concessive ('those concerns should be taken seriously', 'I would support a serious effort... so long as...', scaling for smaller issuers, 'unless it can demonstrate with strong evidence'); the alternative-reform framing is exactly the pattern that flips Skeptic to Conditional. Majority still Oppose.
rater_notes.rationale: Richest form letter to date and the FIRST with coded rationales: IA (informational domination/redistribution), IP (public transparency floor), CMP (comparability across issuers — rare among retail), MF (liquidity/price discovery/real-time risk), AL (narrower-reform alternatives). ST and short-termism + CB compliance-burden are acknowledged as pro-side concerns to take seriously but are conceded, not the commenter's own Oppose rationale (inclusive-only).

types.text:
Dear SEC, I oppose the SEC proposal because it prevents investors like me from accessing information about companies; lets companies hide behind closed doors; and allows fraud to fester.

Sincerely,

rater_notes.primary: Oppose — explicit 'I oppose the SEC proposal'; argues retail info-access (IA), concealment/transparency (IP), and fraud (FR).
rater_notes.literalist: Oppose — verbatim 'I oppose.' Codes IA/IP/FR all surface-explicit ('accessing information', 'hide behind closed doors', 'fraud to fester').
rater_notes.skeptic: Oppose — unconditional; no hedge or alternative cadence.
rater_notes.rationale: Coded 2026-06-18 per Tzachi (previously logged 'in substance' only): IA (retail access), FR (fraud), IP (concealment/transparency). Unanimous across raters.

rationale_evidence.IA: prevents investors like me from accessing information about companies (retail access disadvantage vs better-positioned actors)
rationale_evidence.IP: lets companies hide behind closed doors (loss of public transparency)

types.text:
The proposal to abandon quarterly reporting in favor of semiannual reports should NOT move forward. The proposal rests on the theory that quarterly reporting causes short-termism among corporate managers. Whatever the merits of that argument as applied to the C-suite, it has the relationship to retail investors exactly backward. Quarterly reports are the single most important leveling mechanism between retail and institutional investors in U.S. equity markets. Institutional investors have expert networks, channel checks, alternative data, satellite imagery of retailer parking lots, credit card panel data, and direct management access through conferences and one-on-one meetings that cost more than most of our portfolios. We have the 10-Q.
There are several issues:
Quarterly reporting protects individual investors. Semiannual reporting doubles the information gap between insiders and ordinary shareholders. That gap is where volatility, uncertainty, and mistrust grow.
The short-termism diagnosis doesn't justify this remedy. Research from the CFA Institute, which studied this exact maneuver in the U.K. market, shows that reporting frequency has no measurable effect on corporate investment behavior. The SEC is accepting a real cost – less transparency for individual investors – for a benefit that the evidence says simply won't materialize.
The companies most likely to opt out are the ones where oversight matters most. Smaller, less-covered businesses operate with less scrutiny. Reducing their reporting requirements makes them harder to monitor, not easier to own.
This is the wrong direction. Reg FD moved toward a more level playing field. This proposal moves away from it.
What, exactly, is the problem? As the WallStreetBets comment letter put it: "The companies we trade are not being held back from greatness by the obligation to file four reports a year. ... The entire S&P 500 files a 10-Q every quarter, and the S&P 500 is at an all-time high. If quarterly reporting is crushing American capitalism, American capitalism is hiding it well."
Simply put, the proposed amendment should NOT be allowed to advance.

rationale_evidence.IA: Quarterly reports are the single most important leveling mechanism between retail and institutional investors; semiannual doubles the information gap between insiders and ordinary shareholders. Institutions have expert networks, channel checks, alternative data, satellite imagery, credit-card panels, and management access; retail has the 10-Q.
rationale_evidence.FR: The companies most likely to opt out are smaller, less-covered businesses where oversight matters most; reducing their reporting makes them harder to monitor.
rationale_evidence.MF: The widened information gap is where volatility, uncertainty, and mistrust grow.
rationale_evidence.ICc: CFA Institute study of the U.K. market shows reporting frequency has no measurable effect on corporate investment behavior — the short-termism remedy's benefit will not materialize.

rater_notes.primary: Oppose — flat rejection ('should NOT move forward', 'should NOT be allowed to advance'); the retail-vs-institutional leveling argument is the spine, with a UK-evidence rebuttal of the short-termism premise.
rater_notes.literalist: Oppose — explicit 'should NOT move forward' / 'should NOT be allowed to advance.'
rater_notes.skeptic: Oppose — the 'whatever the merits... as applied to the C-suite' line is a rhetorical concession, not a conditional; NO alternative cadence or 'so long as' structure (contrast Type E). Nothing to flip to Conditional.
rater_notes.rationale: Substantive template; codes IA (retail-vs-institutional leveling / information gap — central), IP (protects individual investors), ICc (CFA/UK evidence that frequency has no effect on investment — rebuts short-termism), FR (smaller, less-covered companies harder to monitor), MF (volatility/uncertainty from the data gap). Literalist omits FR ('harder to monitor', not explicit fraud) and ICc (UK citation framed as a no-effect rebuttal rather than the code's cautionary-hides-problems framing) -> majority, not unanimous.

render_spec.text_cell: Long template — link out to the SEC 'typeg' aggregate URL (s7202615-typeg.htm) instead of inlining (same as D/E).

types.text:
I am writing to express my strong opposition to the Commission's proposal to make quarterly reporting optional and allow public companies to switch to a semiannual schedule via Form 10-S.

Reducing disclosure frequency will severely damage the transparency, efficiency, and fairness of the U.S. capital markets. I urge the Commission to reject this change based on three critical concerns:

Increased Information Asymmetry: A six-month reporting gap is far too long in today's fast-moving markets. Depriving investors of timely data will widen the gap between corporate insiders and the public, fueling market speculation, rumors, and heightened volatility.

The Inadequacy of Voluntary Releases: Unstructured, voluntary quarterly press releases on Form 8-K are no substitute for Form 10-Q. They often lack essential financial statements and MD&A disclosures. Furthermore, allowing companies to choose different reporting schedules creates a fragmentation of disclosure quality, destroying industry comparability for analysts and investors.

No Material Cost Savings or Strategic Benefits: The administrative savings for companies are highly speculative, as lenders and debt covenants will still require quarterly financial tracking. Additionally, there is no empirical evidence that reducing transparency cures corporate "short-termism." Short-termism is a governance issue, not a disclosure issue, and it should not be solved by withholding vital data from the public.

The SEC's core mandate is investor protection. Moving to optional semiannual reporting directly compromises this mission and undermines investor confidence. Please withdraw this proposal and maintain the mandatory quarterly reporting framework.

rationale_evidence.IA: A six-month reporting gap will widen the gap between corporate insiders and the public; depriving investors of timely data.
rationale_evidence.MF: Stale data fuels market speculation, rumors, and heightened volatility (price discovery / market quality harm).
rationale_evidence.CMP: Voluntary 8-K press releases lack essential financial statements and MD&A; allowing different reporting schedules creates fragmentation of disclosure quality, destroying industry comparability for analysts and investors.
rationale_evidence.CC: Lenders and debt covenants will still require quarterly financial tracking, so the administrative savings are illusory (contractual constraints).
rationale_evidence.IP: The SEC's core mandate is investor protection; the proposal directly compromises this mission and undermines investor confidence.

rater_notes.primary: Oppose - 'strong opposition'; structured three-concern argument (information asymmetry, inadequacy of voluntary 8-K releases, no real cost savings) and an explicit withdraw demand.
rater_notes.literalist: Oppose - explicit 'strong opposition', 'I urge the Commission to reject this change', 'Please withdraw this proposal and maintain the mandatory quarterly reporting framework.'
rater_notes.skeptic: Oppose - one-directional; the short-termism and cost-savings lines are rebuttals of the pro-side, NOT concessions. No 'so long as', no alternative cadence, no hedge - nothing to flip to Conditional (contrast Type E).
rater_notes.rationale: Substantive template; codes IA (insider-vs-public gap), MF (speculation/volatility from stale data), CMP (8-K lacks 10-Q/MD&A granularity + fragmentation destroys comparability), CC (debt covenants still require quarterly tracking - savings illusory), IP (investor-protection mandate). ST (short-termism) and CB (compliance-cost savings) are pro-side arguments raised only to rebut them, and RI (analyst reliance) - all inclusive-only, so majority not unanimous.

render_spec.text_cell: Medium template (~330 words) - link out to the SEC 'typeh' aggregate URL (s7202615-typeh.htm).

types.text:
Four reasons this is bad for every investor.

01 You'd be flying blind for six months. A lot can go wrong in half a year. Failed product launches, ballooning debt, lawsuits, vanishing customers. Today the law forces companies to surface that information every quarter. Under the new rule, they can sit on it for six months at a stretch. Plenty of time for insiders to quietly sell their shares to you (or your ETF) before the bad news tanks the stock.

02 More time for fraud to grow. Enron. WorldCom. Madoff. The run-up to 2008. Every major modern accounting scandal was enabled by gaps and opacity in financial disclosure. Quarterly filings don't prevent fraud, but they dramatically shorten the window in which it can fester before someone outside the company can see it. What if Enron only had to cook their books twice a year?

03 "Optional" is a trick. The framing is voluntary, but the dynamics aren't. Once large companies start filing twice a year, smaller competitors face pressure to follow. They can't justify "more compliance than the bigger guy." Investor expectations adjust downward. What started as a choice quietly becomes the new normal. Almost no rule like this stays optional for long.

04 The supposed savings are tiny, and they don't go to you. Companies still have to keep their books. They still have to brief their boards. The actual savings from skipping a public filing are modest legal and accounting fees, and those savings flow to the company and its executives. The cost of worse information falls on you, the person whose 401(k), IRA, or college fund holds the stock. That's a bad trade for ordinary investors.

rationale_evidence.FR: 'More time for fraud to grow' - Enron, WorldCom, Madoff, the run-up to 2008; quarterly filings shorten the window in which fraud can fester. Also insiders quietly selling shares before bad news tanks the stock.
rationale_evidence.IA: 'Flying blind for six months' while insiders sell their shares to you (or your ETF) before the bad news - the insider-vs-retail information gap.
rationale_evidence.EX: 'Optional is a trick' - once large companies file twice a year, smaller competitors face pressure to follow and it becomes the new normal (spillover/externality).
rationale_evidence.CB: 'The supposed savings are tiny, and they don't go to you' - modest legal/accounting fees that flow to the company and its executives while the cost of worse information falls on ordinary investors' 401(k)/IRA.

rater_notes.primary: Oppose - 'Four reasons this is bad for every investor'; a flat four-point case (flying blind, fraud window, optional-is-a-trick, savings are tiny) ending 'a bad trade for ordinary investors.'
rater_notes.literalist: Oppose - entirely anti-proposal framing ('bad for every investor', 'bad trade for ordinary investors'); no endorsement language.
rater_notes.skeptic: Oppose - one-directional; four reasons it is bad with no concession, no hybrid/alternative cadence, no 'so long as' - nothing to flip to Conditional.
rater_notes.rationale: Reasons map cleanly: 01 -> IA + FR (flying blind; insiders sell ahead of bad news), 02 -> FR (Enron/WorldCom/Madoff fraud window), 03 -> EX (smaller competitors pressured to follow; new normal), 04 -> CB (savings tiny/flow to execs/cost on retail). MF (price impact), IP (investor-protection umbrella / 401k harm), RI (reliance), and OP (rebutted optionality framing) are inclusive-only -> majority not unanimous.

render_spec.text_cell: Medium template (~330 words, 4 numbered reasons) - link out to the SEC 'typei' aggregate URL (s7202615-typei.htm).
